item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun way to keep your physics computational skills in practice I suppose! Now to find out what large structures can and cannot survive that… purely as a thought experiment, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Enjoyable…though I actually sat down and crunched the basic numbers for how much energy that bowling ball released if going at 930 m/s.

Comes out to approx 2.74 MJ, aka approximately equal to the energy of 0.656 kilograms of TNT.

Edit: Forgot to divide by 2 because I'm a fail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
M'yeah, the conceit doesn't do enough for me, and that's all being presented here. It's clever but the execution rides entirely on that cleverness and the piece loses steam before even reaching the end, despite its brevity.

Side note, one thing that hasn't been mentioned yet is that Singapore is antipodal to Ecuador, not Colombia. This seems like an oversight rather than a deliberate choice, particularly given the importance of antipodes to the premise of the article. Alas, it is minor and yet irksome to a geographer. I assume such an edit wouldn't be permitted in this case, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well as someone who isn't a huge physics fan (but knows enough to get this item) I am decidedly nonplussed by this scip. It's a physics accurate teleporter. That's it. Some folks may love the simplicity of this scip but I guess I like my anomalies more odd, weird or bizarre. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find myself agreeing with this. There's no substance to this article at all. And I'm kinda surprised it's so highly rated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is… nothing. It's completely and utterly nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah… another useless teleporter from Prometheus Labs… at least they didn't intend this one to function as a toy! Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, but why would the foundation cease testing on something so valuable just because they caused some damage? No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless, of course, the redacted text includes the actual street address of the buildings wherein SCP-1290 is housed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, given the small size of my country Singapore (around 700 km square right now if I remember correctly), and given how each teleporter's location is relative to each other, it could make little sense to redact the exact location of the teleporter in Colombia since an educated guess could throw up very few possibilities.

item.title: Admin Post
item.content:encoded:
If you don't like it, you don't like it, but this isn't criticism, it's self-aggrandizing spam and violates our criticism policy. This line of discussion is now closed, feel free to contact me or another staff member with any questions.

EDIT: x-posted with Photo, whoops

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Kinne, you are entitled to your opinion, but you are not entitled to be nasty to other users because of it. Posts like this one, which is content-free except for personal attacks, are unacceptable.

This is your official warning. (Yes, this is a cross-post.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it for the story that you make up on your own of the scientists who made this thing. The addendum also gave me a chuckle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not the biggest fan of this article, but this is not a good post. You're saying virtually nothing about the article beyond a rather hyperbolic expression of its mediocrity. And you cap it off by impugning the standards of everyone who upvoted it. Not cool, Kinne.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not in the slightest bit interesting. I would've had more fun staring at a blank screen than reading this. How does this have a positive score, let alone a score of 83? I guess people are just EASILY amused

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect any other relative changes to momentum besides the object's relation to the Earth would be minor or even negligible.

However, considering the effects of a difference in altitude between the two sites would be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, due to what is suspected to be a design or engineering flaw, the object retains its orientation and velocity relative to the axis of the earth. That is, when a stationary object enters SCP-1290-1, it exits SCP-1290-2 upside-down and travelling east at approximately 930 m/s.

SOLD

Also, I really like the humorous addendum, shockingly. And the use of Prometheus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would fix the lesser of two probelms. You'd still have a bowling ball shooting off thru the wall, but at least it would be right-side up.

What i want to know is if it's an open or closed platform. An enclosed platform is traditional and a very good idea to cut down on interference/contaminates (although it didn't work for Goldblum in The Fly), but an open platform has possibilities to counteract this flaw. A subject moving due west at exactly 465 m/s would arrive with a net velocity of zero. That's fast, but not impossibly fast, the real difficultiy would be triggering the mechanism at the exact right moment. At such high speeds, a few fractions of a second late and you may get a zero-velocity rear-half of the subject.

Afterthought, what if you moved the pods to the north and south poles? If you got the positioning exactly right, the relative velocity in relation to Earth would be zero. Or move one 90 degrees perpendicular to the axis. The subject would arrive…how now? at half speed? shooting off at an angle? I can't picture it anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder how long it will be before one of them suggests turning the receiving teleporter upside down.

+1 for great physics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Angular momentum is a result of directional momentum and gravity. Gravity is a constant force, so when you teleport, gravity changes.

Here, why don't I just leave this here:

http://taenaria.com/Stuff/scp-1290.gif

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way it's phrased makes it sound like momentum is conserved relative to the earth only, so it's motion due to the earth's rotation about its axis and not for example around the sun, which could sent you flying up or down although I'm not sure by how much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Earth as a special frame of reference because we were testing it, teleporting items between two places on earth, duh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't teleport them exactly right it conserves one of several known conserved quantities which a teleporter would have to break, and even that only in reference to a special frame which is something that no known law of physics recognises.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like it, why are we conserving momentum but not angular momentum makes no sense it's supposed to be lol let's apply real physics but it doesn't really just middle school physics. Also why the Earth as a special frame of reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now all Prometheus needs to do is invent a device that suspends inertial mass, while retaining gravitic mass, so a device send through will not cause any damage, until the inertial damper (I'll call it a Bergenholm for short) is turned off.

Punchline here.

(grins, ducks and runs)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mission accomplished. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the horribly hilarious mental image that resulted the moment I read "upside-down and travelling east at approximately 930 m/s".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so simple, and I love the massive "oops" factor involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoted because it fits my headcanon of Prometheus Labs - a company that delves into the paranormal trying so hard to create viable products, but that always end up having something go horribly wrong in the process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love real physics in action! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In one of his stories ("Flash Crowd", I think), Larry Niven went into some detail about the problems of momentum and potential energy involved in long-range teleportation (unless the two terminals are at the same distance from the center of Earth's mass, allowing for the possibility of masscons under one or both platforms, there's going to be a change in potential energy of the item as well). His Jumpshift Corp. used handwave tech to transfer excess momentum to floating platforms in various bodies of water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second one, I think, would work very well.

item.title: Re: Stupid things
item.content:encoded:
Oh, it was a typo? I liked the idea of somebody trying to teleport an object to the same position and having something go terribly wrong.

You know, one of those "Why is there an explicit rule disallowing this?" sort of situations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the point is that it doesn't create a ton of kinetic energy. It preserves the kinetic energy the object already had. In a simplistic sense, it would take more energy to not have the end-product flying away at high velocity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. I'm wondering how much energy is needed to run these things, because it moves things damn fast and creates a ton of kinetic energy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This fits perfectly into my headcannon of Prometheus Labs as a bunch of mad scientists.

Upvoted.

Also, how are any of these pictures for potentially illustrating this SCP?

http://img.gawkerassets.com/img/17h3wbs3pxwezjpg/original.jpg

http://lh3.ggpht.com/DucknetServices/SHJbyzBZkmI/AAAAAAAAIEY/KvsOZdUoDI4/s1600-h/image%5B12%5D.png

http://spectrum.ieee.org/image/1947877

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very clever

item.title: Re: Stupid things
item.content:encoded:
e.e damn you.

Edit: Fixed the typo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me 'too perfect' is the same as 'not quite right', in either scenario it's teleporting things in a way that you (the user) find extremely undesirable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, Ophite got it right. It's a teleporter that teleports things *exactly* right. Folks just don't consciously realize that if it actually needs to be sent through "wrong" in order to come out "correct".

item.title: Stupid things
item.content:encoded:
Foundation researchers attempted to teleport a 14 pound bowling ball from SCP-1290-1 to SCP-1290-1

Hehe, this is the exact sort of stupid thing a wiseass intellectual in such a position would eventually attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fitting for Prometheus Labs and a wide margin for things to go fubar if it were uncontained. Also, simple. I'm beginning to like simple. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could use a picture to spice it up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty boring, a teleporter that teleports things 'not quite right'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not sure I like this. But I like the idea of a common science-fiction object that actually works according to known principles of physics (and thus doesn't work).
